title: The label
checkLabels.t4: You didn't use style on the tag
checkLabels.t6: Exactly one paragraph has the class
checkLabels.t7: The class is on the right paragraph
checkLabels.t10: The heading is still dark blue
why.empty: The editor is empty. Bring the files back and start again.
why.untouched: The code is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: Here you work in BOTH files. The label goes in `index.html`, the rule goes in `styles.css`.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or a quote is missing.
hint2.not-closed: The `class` value goes in quotes: `class="note"`. One to open, one to close.
hint3.not-closed: `
why.unlinked: The page can't find `styles.css`. You've edited the `link` line.
hint2.unlinked: Bring the files back and start again. You're touching the class and the rule, not the link.
why.h1-broken: The heading isn't dark blue any more. You edited the `h1` rule, and that one was fine.
hint2.h1-broken: You're adding a new rule. The old ones stay untouched.
why.used-inline: You used `style` on the tag. That's exactly what we're getting away from — you now have the real tool.
hint2.used-inline: A class is a label, not a style. In the HTML you only put the name. The colour goes in `styles.css`.
why.lost-p: There aren't three paragraphs any more. You're adding a label, not deleting or adding.
hint2.lost-p: All three sentences have to stay. Only one of them gets a `class`.
why.wrong-count: The class is on the wrong number of paragraphs. It should be exactly one.
hint2.wrong-count: You've put it on more than one — or on none.
hint3.wrong-count: Put `class="note"` on all three and all three go grey. The rule works, but you've achieved nothing — they all look the same again, just greyer.
hint4.wrong-count: One class, one paragraph. Remove it from the others.
why.no-note: There's no element with that class. Either you didn't add it, or the name doesn't match.
hint2.no-note: Compare letter by letter: the one in `class="..."` and the one after the dot in `styles.css`.
hint3.no-note: `.Note` and `.note` are different. In a page with `<!DOCTYPE html>` the case counts — a capital in one place and a lowercase in the other means two different classes.
hint4.no-note: In HTML: `class="note"`. In CSS: `.note { }`. No dot in the first, a dot in the second.
why.wrong-one: The class is on the wrong paragraph. The note is the one about Christmas.
hint2.wrong-one: Which of the three sentences is extra information rather than part of the main text?
why.note-not-gray: The paragraph with the class isn't grey.
hint2.note-not-gray: The rule starts with a dot and the class name: `.note { }`
hint3.note-not-gray: If you wrote `note` without a dot, CSS looks for a tag called note. There isn't one, so the rule catches nothing.
why.note-not-italic: The note is grey but not italic.
hint2.note-not-italic: The property is `font-style`. It goes in the same rule.
hint3.note-not-italic: `font-style: italic;` — that slants the text. Don't confuse it with `font-weight`, which makes it bold.
blocks.1.text: Last lesson you tried to reach one paragraph and couldn't. A tag selector knows what a thing IS, not which one it is. What you were missing was a way to PUT A LABEL on it.
blocks.3.text: First you stick the label in the HTML. The attribute is called `class`, and the name is yours to pick:
blocks.5.text: Then you call it in the CSS. A dot in front — it says "this is a class, not a tag":
blocks.7.text: The dot is the difference. `note` without a dot means a tag called note — there isn't one. `.note` means any element carrying that label.
blocks.9.text: The name says WHAT the thing is, not how it looks. `note` is a good name. `gray` is a bad one — tomorrow you decide the note should be blue, and your class is called gray. The rest of the code is now lying.
blocks.10.text: One element can carry several classes, separated by spaces: `class="note urgent"`. And one class can sit on as many elements as you like — that's exactly what makes it useful. Put `note` on five notes across the whole site and fix all five with one rule.
blocks.11.text: Selectors stick together in the CSS too, with no space between them. `p.note` means a paragraph carrying that class — not every element with it. `.note.urgent` means an element carrying BOTH labels. A space between them would mean something else entirely, and that's lesson 11.
blocks.12.text: A class isn't a style. It's a name. The style lives separately, in the CSS — and that's precisely why one label can mean different things on different pages.
blocks.14.text: Put a class of `note` on the Christmas paragraph — only that one. In `styles.css` make it grey and italic.
blocks.16.text: Don't touch the other rules. And don't put the class on all three — if you do, it'll work, and you'll be right back where you started.
blocks.18.items.0: In HTML: `class="note"`, no dot. In CSS: `.note`, with a dot.
blocks.18.items.1: The name says what the thing IS, not how it looks.
blocks.18.items.2: One class on many elements. One element with many classes.
blocks.18.items.3: Case counts. `.Note` and `.note` are two different classes.
blocks.19.text: One to keep in mind: the name has to start with a letter. `class="2col"` is legal HTML, but `.2col` in CSS doesn't work — and nobody will tell you why. Start your names with a letter and you'll never meet this.
walkthrough.0.text:
The class goes in the opening tag, like any other attribute. The closing `

` isn't touched — it has no attributes at all.
walkthrough.1.text: The `.note` rule sits BELOW the `p` rule. That isn't an accident: the two argue over the colour of the same paragraph, and the lower one wins. Why exactly that one — lesson 53.
walkthrough.2.text: I wrote `gray`. If you wrote `grey`, that passes too — the two names are the same colour in CSS and the browser reports them identically.
walkthrough.3.text: I called it `note`, not `gray` or `last`. `gray` would have started lying the day the note turned blue. `last` would have started lying the day you added a paragraph below it. A name that describes the role outlives a name that describes the look or the position.
